title: Is This Governance, or Merely a Spectacle?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: In yesterday's letter to you, I wrote a paragraph about this government's announced Madurai satellite town and the multi-storey housing at Sholinganallur. I write today to explain the matter in greater detail.

An AIADMK government press release dated 20 October 2013 said the Chief Minister had granted administrative approval of Rs 1,379.51 crore for 1,500 multi-storey flats at Sholinganallur. It also said she had ordered an integrated satellite town near Madurai, with 19,500 plots and every facility, including a school complex, commercial plots, police station, post office, primary health centre, fire station and developed industrial plots.
After listing 19,500 plots and all these facilities, it finally says only Rs 120 crore has initially been approved for basic infrastructure. Let me tell you how many months even that approval took.

On 4 April 2013, the Chief Minister announced under Rule 110 in the Assembly that, to meet Madurai's growing housing need, an integrated satellite town would be created on 586.86 acres belonging to the Tamil Nadu Housing Board at Thoppur and Uchapatti villages, near the airport on the Madurai-Tirunelveli four-lane road, about fifteen kilometres from Madurai. She announced 19,500 plots.
The moment she sat down, representatives of the Republican Party of India, Forward Bloc, All India Samathuva Makkal Katchi and Communist Party of India rose to praise her.

Six months after that grand announcement, the government has only now granted an initial Rs 120 crore administrative approval for infrastructure. Is another example needed of the theatre of deception conducted by this government?

Soon after the AIADMK came to office, Jayalalithaa read another Rule 110 statement on 8 September 2011. Amid great fanfare she announced that the Housing Board would establish a Thirumazhisai satellite town on 311 acres at a cost of Rs 2,160 crore, with 12,000 flats affordable to economically weaker, low-income and middle-income groups.

Two years have passed. Where are those 12,000 flats? There is another curious detail. Housing Minister R. Vaithilingam had already announced the scheme in his policy note on 25 August 2011. The Chief Minister merely read it again under Rule 110. I pointed this out then, but as usual no one offered an explanation. The AIADMK government never properly answers the many issues I raise in the public interest and for the State's progress. Does that not show that it lacks the basis for an answer and is indifferent to the country's welfare?

The idea of a satellite town near Chennai did not originate with this AIADMK government. The DMK government proposed one in 2006. An Assembly announcement on 1 September 2006 said that south of the Vandalur-Kelambakkam road lay 140,000 acres, of which the Chennai Metropolitan Development Authority had tentatively identified 30,000. About 13,000 families lived in the 270-square-kilometre area, which had a clean environment suitable for a new town. Most land was uncultivated. Water resources, settlements and forests would be excluded, and only dry and fallow land acquired at fair market prices. Residents would not be displaced and could continue living where they were; new employment would improve their economic condition.

The very next day, 2 September 2006, Jayalalithaa hurriedly issued a statement. She called the proposal another example of anti-democratic, anti-people disorder under "arrogant, petty creatures," accused me of lacking foresight, and said I and my party would have to pay the price. She then attacked my family in degrading terms, saying that before reducing Chennai's congestion I should send my children, grandchildren, relatives and extended family out of Gopalapuram and CIT Colony. She made a crude play on the word "satellite" or "companion," and alleged that the project was designed to benefit my family and grandsons Dayanidhi and Kalanithi Maran.

That is how recklessly and abusively she wrote about me. Today, how many defamation cases has she filed against opposition parties? Yet although her words against a serving Chief Minister were repulsive, the DMK government did not file a defamation case. Even with reasonable grounds, we regarded criticism according to each politician's standards as normal in politics.

Forgetting that statement, Jayalalithaa later announced a satellite town at Thirumazhisai and another at Madurai. I am not prepared to imitate her by asking whether she now loves "companions" so much that she seeks one satellite town after another.

Before Jayalalithaa opposed the DMK proposal, Dr Ramadoss of the PMK, then our ally, had expressed in civil language the concern that farmland and residents would suffer. Within two days, on 2 September 2006, I announced that although the project had appeared in the DMK manifesto, Governor's Address and Budget, it was being abandoned in goodwill, after considering an ally's advice and to ensure innocent local people did not become victims of violence planned by selfish, malicious forces.
On 3 September, the PMK's official newspaper *Tamil Osai* published an article titled "Jayalalithaa's Statement: The Summit of Indecency."

On 4 September, *Theekkathir*, newspaper of the CPI(M), which now supports the AIADMK in the Yercaud by-election, wrote that Chief Minister Karunanidhi had withdrawn the satellite-town plan in goodwill and respect for allies. It contrasted this with the previous AIADMK government's arrogant refusal to abandon its attempt to demolish Queen Mary's College for a Secretariat despite student and public protests. It said opposition parties had every right to oppose a scheme but must preserve political decency, whereas Jayalalithaa had used repulsive language about my family. Her hostility and perversity were apparently unbearable even to AIADMK members when I read the statement in the Assembly. Legislatures and political forums, it said, should host civil debate, not anger and slander, and her words showed that she and her party had learnt nothing from the people's verdict.
I cannot help reminding our Marxist comrades of what *Theekkathir* wrote then.

What has become of the Thirumazhisai satellite town announced in 2011? After two years, not even a foundation stone appears to have been laid. Yet a Madurai satellite town announced six months ago has now received administrative approval after six months. Moving at a tortoise's pace like many AIADMK schemes, when will it be implemented?

Is one announcement issued to unveil a scheme and another for administrative approval merely a device to deceive the people? The extraordinary spectacle of forgetting necessary public work, seeking publicity through announcements and passing the time continues to be staged.
Is Tamil Nadu being governed, or is only a spectacle being performed? Could they at least tell us that?
With affection,
M. K.
23 October 2013
